title: Insects & Minibeasts
objective: Students will explore insects & minibeasts concepts including The insect body plan, Not all minibeasts are insects, Sorting and Identifying Minibeasts and more.

steps.content: Let's discover the amazing world of The insect body plan!

The insect body plan: all insects share three body parts (head, thorax, abdomen), six legs attached to the thorax, and antennae on the head. Most have wings. They have an exoskeleton — a hard outer shell — instead of bones inside.
Time to try this out: Label the three body parts of an insect — head, thorax, and abdomen — on a diagram or real specimen
If you looked at a picture of a beetle or ant, could they point out the head, thorax, and abdomen, and tell you that all insects have six legs and a hard outer shell?

Great job! Today we learned about The insect body plan. Label the three body parts of an insect — head, thorax, and abdomen — on a diagram or real specimen State that all insects have exactly six legs and that the legs attach to the thorax
Let's explore Not all minibeasts are insects! Have you ever wondered about this?

Not all minibeasts are insects: distinguishing insects from other minibeasts. Spiders have 8 legs and 2 body parts (arachnids), woodlice have 14 legs (crustaceans), worms have no legs, snails have a shell and one foot. The 'Is it an insect?' sorting game.
Let me show you something cool. Sort a set of minibeasts into 'insect' and 'not insect' groups using the six-legs rule
If you showed you a spider and asked whether it's an insect, could they explain why not — for example, that spiders have eight legs, not six?

Great job! Today we learned about Not all minibeasts are insects. Sort a set of minibeasts into 'insect' and 'not insect' groups using the six-legs rule Explain why a spider is not an insect by noting it has eight legs and two body parts
Let's explore Sorting and Identifying Minibeasts! Have you ever wondered about this?

Using classification keys to identify minibeasts. Branching yes/no questions: 'Does it have legs?' → 'How many legs?' → 'Does it have wings?' Dichotomous keys as a systematic tool for sorting and identifying creatures.
Let's practice together! Follow a simple branching key to correctly identify at least four different minibeasts
If you found an unfamiliar minibeast, could they use a set of yes/no questions — like 'Does it have legs? How many?' — to work out what group it belongs to?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Sorting and Identifying Minibeasts: Follow a simple branching key to correctly identify at least four different minibeasts Create a yes/no question that separates insects from spiders, such as 'Does it have six legs?'
Let's explore Social insects: ants and bees! Have you ever wondered about this?

Social insects: how ants and bees live and work together in colonies. Queens, workers, and drones. Division of labour — some gather food, some build, some guard. Ant tunnels and bee hives as organised homes. Parallels to human teamwork.
Let's practice together! Describe at least two different roles within an ant colony or bee hive such as queen, worker, or guard
If you watched a nature programme about ants or bees, could they tell you about the different jobs inside the colony — like the queen, the workers, and the guards?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Social insects: ants and bees. Describe at least two different roles within an ant colony or bee hive such as queen, worker, or guard Explain that social insects live together in large groups and divide up the jobs needed to survive
Let's explore Bees and pollination! Have you ever wondered about this?

Bees and pollination: how flowers and insects depend on each other. Bees visit flowers for nectar, pollen sticks to their bodies and transfers to the next flower. Without pollination many plants cannot make seeds or fruit. Why bees matter for the food we eat.
Time to try this out: Describe how pollen moves from one flower to another when a bee visits to collect nectar
If you saw a bee buzzing around flowers in the garden, could they explain how the bee is helping the plant — and why that matters for the food we eat?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Bees and pollination. Describe how pollen moves from one flower to another when a bee visits to collect nectar Explain that many fruits and vegetables depend on bees or other insects for pollination
Let's discover the amazing world of Camouflage, warning colours, and mimicry!

Camouflage, warning colours, and mimicry: how insects survive by hiding or sending visual signals. Stick insects look like twigs, leaf insects look like leaves. Wasps have warning stripes; hoverflies mimic wasps but are harmless. The 'can you spot it?' challenge.
Time to try this out: Give at least two examples of insect camouflage such as stick insects resembling twigs or leaf insects resembling leaves
If you showed you pictures of stick insects hiding on branches or hoverflies pretending to be wasps, could they explain why those insects look the way they do?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Camouflage, warning colours, and mimicry: Give at least two examples of insect camouflage such as stick insects resembling twigs or leaf insects resembling leaves Explain why bright warning colours like a wasp's yellow and black stripes help the insect survive
Let's discover the amazing world of Insect life cycles: complete metamorphosis!

Insect life cycles — complete metamorphosis in detail. Egg → larva → pupa → adult. The larva (caterpillar, grub, maggot) looks completely different from the adult. Inside the pupa the body is rebuilt. Butterflies, beetles, flies, and ladybirds all undergo complete metamorphosis.
Let's practice together! Name the four stages of complete metamorphosis using the correct terms: egg, larva, pupa, adult
Can you explain the full life cycle of a beetle or butterfly using the proper words — egg, larva, pupa, adult — and tell you what happens at each stage?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Insect life cycles: complete metamorphosis: Name the four stages of complete metamorphosis using the correct terms: egg, larva, pupa, adult Explain that the larva stage is when the insect eats and grows, and the pupa stage is when its body transforms
Today we're going to learn about Incredible insects: record-breakers. Get ready for an adventure!

Incredible insects — record-breakers and superpowers. Dung beetles are the strongest animals relative to body weight. Dragonflies are among the fastest flying insects. Fleas can jump over 150 times their own body length. Bombardier beetles spray boiling chemicals. The 'wow factor' of the insect world.
Let's practice together! Name at least three insect record-breakers and their extreme abilities such as dung beetle strength or flea jumping
Can you tell you about insect record-breakers — like which insect is the world's strongest for its size, or which one can jump the farthest?

Great job! Today we learned about Incredible insects: record-breakers. Name at least three insect record-breakers and their extreme abilities such as dung beetle strength or flea jumping Compare an insect's ability to a human scale, for example a flea's jump would be like a person leaping over a skyscraper
